title: Why Crypto Rails Are the Default Plumbing for Agent-to-Agent Payments

source.original: The Open Agentic Commerce Stack – a16z crypto (Eddy Lazzarin, Sam Ragsdale, Noah Levine, Robert Hackett)

body_html:
When AI agents need to transact autonomously — paying for services, purchasing data, settling micro-negotiations — they need payment infrastructure that matches their operational characteristics: programmable, permissionless, and capable of real-time settlement without human intervention. Traditional financial rails, built around identity verification, batch processing, and institutional gatekeeping, are a poor fit.

Blockchain-based payment systems offer a compelling alternative precisely because they were designed for exactly this kind of interaction. Smart contracts can encode complex payment logic — escrow, conditional release, split payments — that agents can invoke without needing a bank account, a credit card, or a human co-signer. The programmability is native, not bolted on.

Permissionlessness is the other critical property. An AI agent spun up by a small developer should be able to transact with an agent deployed by a Fortune 500 company without either party needing to pre-negotiate access to a shared payment platform. Open crypto rails provide this by default — any entity with a wallet address can send and receive value, regardless of identity or institutional affiliation.

This positions crypto infrastructure not as a speculative asset class but as essential plumbing for the machine economy. The same properties that made blockchain awkward for consumer payments — no chargebacks, irreversibility, pseudonymity — become features rather than bugs when the transacting parties are software agents operating at machine speed.

The a16z crypto team's framing is deliberate: if agent commerce scales on closed, proprietary payment platforms, it risks recreating the very walled-garden dynamics that have plagued the current internet. Open, interoperable crypto protocols are presented as the structural antidote.
